paper_title: AUDITFLOW: Executable Symbolic Environments for Structured Financial Reporting Verification

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Structured financial audit verification is difficult for language-model agents because correctness depends on structured evidence rather than text alone. A model must link reported facts to taxonomy concepts, traverse calculation or dimensional relations, and recompute expected values before applying an audit rule. We propose AuditFlow, a graph-grounded multi-agent framework that separates adaptive search from deterministic verification. AuditFlow builds a symbolic environment from a static US-GAAP taxonomy graph and a dynamic XBRL filing graph, and exposes it through typed tools for fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, and rule evaluation. Two junior auditors inspect each case from regulatory and evidentiary views, while a senior auditor resolves disagreements and can request further investigation. The final reports are fused through evidential aggregation to produce an audit verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score. On a FinAuditing-derived FinMR sample, AuditFlow reaches 82.09% joint audit accuracy under GPT-5.5, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.93 points. Removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91%, showing that the symbolic environment performs the verification step that the model cannot reliably replace.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: AUDITFLOW: Executable Symbolic Environments for Structured Financial Reporting Verification Yan Wang1, Xuguang Ai1, Jaisal Patel2, Xueqing Peng1, Fengran Mo3, Yupeng Cao4, Haohang Li4, Mingyu Cao5, Lingfei Qian1,*, Víctor Gutiérrez-Basulto6 1The Fin AI, USA, 2Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, USA, 3Université de Montréal, Canada, 4Stevens Institute of Technology, USA, 5University of Surrey, UK, 6Cardiff University, UK Correspondence: lfqian94@gmail.com 6 2 0 2 2 ] A . [ 1 1 3 0 3 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Structured financial audit verification is difficult for language-model agents because correctness depends on structured evidence rather than text alone. model must link reported facts to taxonomy concepts, traverse calculation or dimensional relations, and recompute expected values before applying an audit rule. We propose AUDITFLOW, graph-grounded multiagent framework that separates adaptive search from deterministic verification. AUDITFLOW builds symbolic environment from static USGAAP taxonomy graph and dynamic XBRL filing graph, and exposes it through typed tools for fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, and rule evaluation. Two junior auditors inspect each case from regulatory and evidentiary views, while senior auditor resolves disagreements and can request further investigation. The final reports are fused through evidential aggregation to produce an audit verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score. On FinAuditing-derived FinMR sample, AUDITFLOW reaches 82.09% joint audit accuracy under GPT-5.5, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14.93 points. Removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91%, showing that the symbolic environment performs the verification step that the model cannot reliably replace.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Public companies disclose financial reports that are used by investors, regulators, and auditors to assess performance and compliance. Many of these reports are filed in eXtensible Business Reporting Language (XBRL), where each reported number is linked to an accounting concept, period, unit, and context. Verifying such number is not just matter of finding it in the filing. The system must decide whether it is consistent with the relevant taxonomy constraints and related facts. Figure 1: An example of the financial audit verification. Figure 1, single audit rule may require linking dispersed XBRL documents, traversing taxonomy relations, recomputing an expected value, and applying rules over structured evidence. These steps are operations over structured artifact, not only text understanding. We call this artifact, together with the typed operations defined over it, symbolic environment. In this setting, correctness is not stored in one sentence of the filing. It depends on how reported facts interact with the structures that constrain them. Recent results show that this kind of verification remains difficult for Large Language Models (LLMs). On the FinAuditing benchmark (Wang et al., 2026), the strongest evaluated model reaches only 13.86% accuracy on the numerical-consistency task. Similar challenges appear in legal compliance and clinical guideline checking, where answers depend on external rules and structured evidence rather than text alone (Zhang et al., 2026b; Liu et al., 2026a). This raises the question we study in this paper: how can language-model agents make reliable decisions when correctness requires interaction with structured symbolic environment? This makes XBRL audit verification useful testbed for financial AI agents. As illustrated in Prior work has made progress, but much of the verification burden still remains inside the language 1 model. Tool-augmented agents (Yao et al., 2023; Schick et al., 2023; Patil et al., 2024) allow models to call external tools, but tool use and tool-output validation often remain driven by natural-language reasoning (Liu et al., 2026b; Yin et al., 2026). Retrievaland graph-augmented methods (Edge et al., 2024; Amayuelas et al., 2025) provide external evidence, and neuro-symbolic methods check reasoning steps against constraints (Feng et al., 2025). Multi-agent reflection and debate frameworks (Fatemi and Hu, 2024; Lee et al., 2026) add cross-checking. Yet these approaches often still require the model to perform the final interpretation, arithmetic, or rule application. Financialdomain systems and benchmarks, including XBRLAgent (Han et al., 2024), FinReporting (Zhang et al., 2026a), Herculean (Peng et al., 2026), and FinRule-Bench (Malarkkan et al., 2026), further expose this gap, but they do not treat the filing and taxonomy as one environment for verification. We instantiate this idea in AUDITFLOW, graphgrounded multi-agent framework for XBRL audit verification. AUDITFLOW separates search from computation: LLM agents decide where to look, while symbolic environment performs fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checks, and rule evaluation. The environment links static US-GAAP taxonomy graph with dynamic filing evidence graph. Two junior auditors inspect the same case from regulatory and evidentiary views, senior auditor resolves disagreements when needed, and evidential aggregation produces the final verdict, expected value, evidence trail, and trustworthiness score. We evaluate AUDITFLOW on FinAuditing FinMR subset (Wang et al., 2026) covering three Data Quality Committee rule families. Under GPT5.5, AUDITFLOW reaches 82.09% joint audit accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline, Single Agent, by 14.93 points. Removing deterministic checks drops accuracy to 17.91% and raises invalid outputs to 35.82%, showing that the symbolic environment performs the load-bearing verification. Results are stable across strong backbones, with GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Qwen-397B each reaching 80.60% joint accuracy. Our contributions are as follows: (1) We define graph-grounded numerical consistency verification for XBRL auditing, where the answer depends on filing facts, taxonomy constraints, numerical checks, and inspectable evidence. (2) We propose AUDITFLOW, dual-graph multi-agent framework that connects US-GAAP taxonomy knowledge with filing-specific evidence and exposes both through typed deterministic tools. (3) We show that search-computation separation is crucial for reliable audit verification: LLM agents guide the search, while deterministic symbolic operations determine the verdict.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Agents, grounding, and verification. LLM agents use tools, retrieval, graphs, and multi-agent interaction to improve over one-step generation. ReAct, Toolformer, and Gorilla study tool use at different scales (Yao et al., 2023; Schick et al., 2023; Patil et al., 2024), while graph-based retrieval and neuro-symbolic methods ground or validate model reasoning with external structure (Edge et al., 2024; Amayuelas et al., 2025; Feng et al., 2025). Multi-agent methods add reflection, debate, or safety-gated collaboration in financial, regulatory, and clinical settings (Fatemi and Hu, 2024; Lee et al., 2026; Agarwal et al., 2025; Mo et al., 2026a; Zhang et al., 2026b; Liu et al., 2026a). These approaches improve grounding, but the final computation, rule application, or trust judgment often remains inside the language model (Liu et al., 2026b; Mo et al., 2026b; Yin et al., 2026). AUDITFLOW instead makes these verification steps executable in the structured environment. LLM agents for financial auditing. Recent financial LLM systems use retrieval, tools, graphs, or agents to analyze filings and disclosures (Han et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025; Arun et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2026a; Peng et al., 2026). Related audit studies also use knowledge graphs and graph neural networks to model accounting structure and detect suspicious entries (Zhong et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2026). Benchmarks such as FinAuditing, FinRuleBench, and FinVault show that numerical consistency, rule attribution, and safe financial-agent execution remain difficult for current models (Wang et al., 2026; Malarkkan et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2026). These works motivate reliable audit verification, but they do not treat the filing and taxonomy as unified executable environment. AUDITFLOW fills this gap by combining dual graph, typed deterministic tools, and role-specialized agents for auditable verification.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: AUDITFLOW follows the principle introduced in Section 1: LLMs guide the search, while the symbolic environment performs the computation. In our setting, the symbolic environment consists of dual graph and set of typed deterministic tools. The graph stores taxonomy constraints and filing evidence, while the tools retrieve facts, traverse relations, perform numerical checks, and evaluate rules. Given an audit query, agents decide what to inspect next and interact with this environment through tool calls. As shown in Figure 2, AUDITFLOW has four components. First, dual-graph audit environment represents the static US-GAAP taxonomy and the dynamic evidence from an XBRL filing. Second, typed action-observation interface exposes the environment through deterministic tools. Third, three-agent audit protocol uses two junior auditors to inspect the same case from different views, while senior auditor reviews their reports and sends the case back for further investigation when needed. Finally, an evidential aggregation step combines the grounded reports into the final audit output. 3.1 Task Formulation We study graph-grounded numerical consistency verification. Given an XBRL filing F, target concept c, reporting period t, and the corresponding US-GAAP taxonomy release Ty, the system decides whether the reported value vrep is consistent with the value implied by the taxonomy, filing context, and applicable audit rule. The system outputs (cid:0)ˆy, vrep, vexp, P, Z, τ (cid:1), (1) where ˆy {consistent, violation} is the final decision, vexp is the expected value computed by deterministic checks, is the action path, is the supporting evidence, and τ is the trustworthiness score from evidential aggregation. We include the action path and evidence in the output because the verdict must be inspectable. rule families: We evaluate three Data Quality Committee (DQC)1 sign consistency (DQC.US.0015), dimensional aggregation consistency (DQC.US.0117), and calculation-tree consistency (DQC.US.0126). These rules require information from reported facts, contexts, units, 1https://www.fasb.org/projects/ fasb-taxonomies/dqc-rules-taxonomy taxonomy metadata, calculation relations, and dimensional structures. AUDITFLOW exposes these sources through executable tools, so the model selects structured operations rather than producing the verdict from text alone. 3.2 Dual-Graph Audit Environment The environment keeps taxonomy knowledge and filing evidence separate. The taxonomy defines the reporting constraints, while the filing provides the reported facts. We represent them as dual-graph environment: = (Gy , GF , A, O), (2) where Gy is the static taxonomy graph for year y, GF is the filing-specific graph, is the typed action space, and O(a, s) is the structured observation returned by action under audit state s. The graph component is = Gy GF , (3) with bridge edges linking reported facts to their taxonomy concepts. Static regulatory graph. The static graph Gy encodes the US-GAAP taxonomy. Its nodes are taxonomy concepts, with metadata such as label, definition, data type, period type, abstract flag, and balance attribute. Its edges encode presentation, calculation, and dimensional relationships. This graph provides the constraints used by the audit tools, including concept lookup, sign checking, calculation traversal, and dimensional validation. Dynamic filing graph. The dynamic graph GF encodes the XBRL filing. Its nodes represent reported facts, contexts, periods, units, and dimensional assignments. fact node stores the concept name, value, unit, decimals, period signature, and dimensional context. Edges connect facts to their contexts, units, dimensions, related facts, and taxonomy concepts. The bridge between the two graphs supports the audit trajectory. The system can start from reported fact, move to the taxonomy concept that governs it, retrieve the relevant calculation or dimensional structure, and return to the filing graph to collect the facts needed for verification. This allows the final output to identify the value, concept, context, relation, and rule involved in the decision. 3 Figure 2: Overview of AUDITFLOW. 3.3 Typed Action and Observation Interface Agents interact with the dual graph through typed tools rather than free-form graph operations. Each tool has fixed name, structured arguments, and deterministic implementation. The LLM selects the tool and arguments, while the environment executes the query or check and returns structured observation. This makes each audit step an actionobservation pair. Tool access follows the agent roles (cf. Section 3.4 below). The Compliance Auditor receives the static taxonomy tools, since it checks the filing against taxonomy constraints. The Forensic Auditor receives both static and dynamic tools, since it must inspect reported facts together with their regulatory context. The Senior Auditor does not call tools in the production setting; it reads the junior reports and issues feedback when needed. The tools cover four operation types. Retrieval tools obtain concept metadata, reported facts, contexts, units, and related evidence. Traversal tools follow calculation, presentation, and dimensional structures. Forensic tools compare facts across periods, dimensions, related concepts, units, and magnitude patterns. Deterministic checkers execute the terminal rule-specific checks for sign consistency, calculation-tree consistency, and dimensional aggregation. These checkers return the triggered rule, reported value, expected value when available, supporting facts, and rule-specific findings; the dimensional checker also returns candidate axis-group sums and an ambiguity flag. The production system treats checker outputs as the authority for final verification, rather than allowing the LLM to override them through self-reflection. Full tool access and tool definitions are provided in Appendix A. 3.4 Three-Agent Audit Protocol AUDITFLOW uses three-agent protocol, where the Compliance Auditor focuses on taxonomy constraints, the Forensic Auditor focuses on filing evidence, and Senior Auditor then reviews their reports and resolves disagreements when needed. Junior auditors. Each junior auditor runs an observe-decide-act loop. At step s, the auditor observes the current audit state os, chooses an action as, receives structured observation os+1 = O(as, s), and appends it to its evidence trace. The action space includes tool_call, ask_senior, and finalize. tool_call queries the environment or invokes deterministic checker. An ask_senior action requests arbitration. finalize action returns structured report with the decision, triggered rules, vrep, vexp, rationale, confidence, and interaction history. The Compliance Auditor A1 is specification-first. It starts from taxonomy metadata, calculation structure, dimensional axes, and applicable rule families, then checks the reported facts against the identified constraints. The Forensic Auditor A2 is evidencefirst. It starts from reported facts, period comparisons, dimensional breakdowns, sibling arithmetic, and unit or decimal consistency, then confirms the result with the same deterministic DQC checks. Thus, A1 and A2 use different search policies over the same environment. For A1, Required-tool gate. junior report is accepted only after the mandatory deterministic checks return non-error observations. the gate requires check_sign, check_calc_tree, and check_dim_consistency. For A2, it additionally requires get_fact_history to ensure that the evidence-first path is exercised. If junior tries to stop early, the environment rejects the action and returns the missing tools as the next observation. This prevents verdict from being finalized before the required evidence has been collected. Senior auditor. The Senior Auditor As receives the two junior reports and their evidence traces. If the juniors agree on the decision and triggered rules, the senior accepts the consensus and the sysIf they tem proceeds to evidential aggregation. disagree, the senior identifies the likely source of conflict, such as missing fact, wrong period, incomplete calculation neighborhood, or unresolved dimensional context. It then returns targeted feedback, and the juniors re-enter the environment for another bounded round. The senior manages the interaction rather than replacing the deterministic tools. Disagreements are resolved by collecting additional evidence from the environment, not by asking the senior to vote from free-form reasoning. 3.5 Evidential Aggregation AUDITFLOW derives the final verdict from structured evidence rather than from an LLM confidence score. Each junior report is mapped to mass function over = {v, c}, where denotes VIOLATION and denotes CONSISTENT, plus an ignorance state Θ: m(v) + m(c) + m(Θ) = 1. (4) If deterministic rule fires, the report assigns mass to VIOLATION. If no rule fires and the required checks are complete, it assigns mass to CONSISTENT. Incomplete or ambiguous evidence leaves more mass on Θ. Let m1 and m2 be the masses from A1 and A2. We combine them using evidential reasoning (Yang and Xu, 2013). For each singleton {v, c}, m(X) = m1(X)m2(X) + m1(X)m2(Θ) + m1(Θ)m2(X), m(Θ) = m1(Θ)m2(Θ), = m1(v)m2(c) + m1(c)m2(v), (5) (6) (7) Algorithm 1 Environment-grounded Multi-Agent Auditing Require: Audit environment = (Gy , GF , A, O), query (c, t), maximum rounds Ensure: ˆy, vrep, vexp, P, Z, τ 1: Initialize shared audit state from query (c, t) 2: Retrieve reported value vrep from GF 3: for = 1 to do 4: Run Compliance Auditor A1 and Forensic Auditor A2 over Enforce required-tool gates before accepting junior reports if A1 and A2 agree on decision and triggered rules 5: 6: then return AGGREGATE(A1, A2) end if feedback As(A1, A2) Append feedback to the shared audit state 7: 8: 9: 10: 11: end for 12: return AGGREGATE(A1, A2) where is the conflict mass. The normalized fused mass is m() = m() 1 . We define the trustworthiness score as τ = 1 m(Θ). (8) (9) For the final binary decision, the remaining ignorance mass is redistributed over VIOLATION and CONSISTENT. The system predicts VIOLATION when the redistributed violation mass is larger. This aggregation exposes two signals: measures disagreement between the junior auditors, and τ measures how much evidence remains unresolved after fusion. 3.6 Bounded Refinement The complete workflow is shown in Algorithm 1. The interaction is bounded by maximum number of refinement rounds K. Within this limit, senior feedback can send the juniors back to collect missing or conflicting evidence. If disagreement remains after the final round, the system still terminates through evidential aggregation. This bounded loop lets the system correct common errors, such as using the wrong period, missing calculation child, skipping required checker, or leaving dimensional context unresolved. In summary, AUDITFLOW separates the main parts of audit verification: the dual graph stores the evidence, the tools execute the checks, the agents guide the search, and evidential aggregation combines the final reports.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: 4.1 Dataset Construction We evaluate AUDITFLOW on 67-instance subset of the FinMR task from FinAuditing (Wang et al., 2026). We use the released benchmark to obtain the tickers, audit queries, target concepts, reporting periods, and ground-truth answers, but re-download the corresponding XBRL filing packages from SEC EDGAR 2 and parse the relevant US-GAAP taxonomy releases independently. This keeps the queries and labels aligned with FinAuditing while requiring AUDITFLOW to verify each case from raw structured filings and taxonomy evidence. More details are in Appendix B. 4.2 Models We evaluate AUDITFLOW with both proprietary and open-weight backbones, including GPT5.5 (OpenAI, 2026), GPT-4o (Hurst et al., 2024), Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic, 2026), Qwen3.5397B-A17B (Qwen Team, 2026a), Qwen3.627B (Qwen Team, 2026b), and Fino1-14B (Qian et al., 2025). This model set allows us to test whether the framework depends on single strong proprietary model or generalizes across different model families and scales. The full model list and providers are given in Appendix C. 4.3 Baselines We compare AUDITFLOW with seven baselines. FinAuditing (Wang et al., 2026) and Herculean (Peng et al., 2026) represent existing financial-auditing benchmark settings. For these two baselines, we use their released benchmark evaluation frameworks and evaluate the models under the best original benchmark settings. Although FinAuditing is also an LLM-based baseline, it evaluates models on manually segmented filing evidence released with the benchmark. In contrast, our Direct LLM (Brown et al., 2020) baseline uses the complete XBRL filing context together with the audit query and asks the model to predict the verdict directly, without retrieval, graph traversal, or deterministic checking. Vanilla RAG (Lewis et al., 2020) retrieves text chunks from filing and taxonomy files, GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024) retrieves graph neighborhoods around the target concept, and TreeRAG (Sarthi et al., 2024) retrieves hierarchical context from taxonomy or filing trees. We also include Single Agent (Yao et al., 2https://www.sec.gov/ 2023) baseline, which uses the same dual-graph environment and tool catalog as AUDITFLOW but replaces the three-agent protocol with one ReActstyle agent. Together, these baselines test whether manually curated evidence, existing agentic benchmark workflows, full-context prompting, retrieval, structured context, or single-agent tool use is sufficient compared with AUDITFLOWs combination of role-specialized multi-agent search and deterministic verification. 4.4 Evaluation Metrics Following FinAuditing (Wang et al., 2026), we evaluate each output as full audit artifact using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol. Our primary metric is joint audit accuracy (Joint ACC), which requires the correct verdict, reported value, and expected value when applicable. We also report FinAuditings structural, extraction, and calculation error rates (SER, EER, CER) to separate format, evidence-extraction, and computation failures. In addition, we report verdict-only accuracy (VAcc) for the binary consistent/violation decision, and use process metrics such as tool-use steps, gate rejections, graph usage, and inter-agent conflict mass for agent-behavior analysis. Full definitions are given in Appendix D. 4.5 Implementation Details AUDITFLOW is implemented as an executable Python pipeline. Unless otherwise stated, the Compliance Auditor, Forensic Auditor, and Senior Auditor use the same backbone in each run to avoid role-specific model confounds. For standard compatible models, we use greedy decoding with temperature 0; junior auditors have 4096-token output budget, while the senior auditor has an 8192token budget because it reads both junior reports. Each junior auditor is limited to 20 ReAct tool steps, and the senior-feedback loop is capped at 3 refinement rounds. In the production setting, the senior is single-shot arbitrator; ReAct senior with 10-step budget is used only for ablation. The offline LLM-as-a-judge is fixed as gpt-5-mini with reasoning_effort=minimal, verbosity=low, and 256-token output budget. Main evaluations are run asynchronously with up to 8 concurrent audit cases. Additional software, backend, hardware, and caching details are provided in Appendix E. 6 Figure 3: Main results across methods and backbone models. Panel (a) reports Joint ACC (%), where higher is better. Panels (b) to (d) report structural error rate (SER), extraction error rate (EER), and calculation error rate (CER), where lower is better.

sections.title: 5 Results
sections.content: 5.1 Main Comparison with Baselines Figure 3 compares AUDITFLOW with seven baselines across six backbone models. AUDITFLOW achieves the best Joint ACC for every backbone, with 82.09% on GPT-5.5, 80.60% on GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Qwen-397B, 73.13% on Qwen-27B, and 31.34% on Fino1-14B. The same trend holds across model families: LLM-only and plain RAG baselines remain weak, GraphRAG and TreeRAG improve with structured context, agentic baselines perform better, and AUDITFLOW is consistently strongest. The comparison also separates structured context from executable verification. GraphRAG and TreeRAG expose graph or hierarchical context, but they still require the model to perform the final rule application and numerical verification. Single Agent and AUDITFLOW instead operate over the dual-graph environment with deterministic tools, which leads to much higher Joint ACC. AUDITFLOW further improves over Single Agent across all backbones, showing that the gain comes not only from tool access, but also from role-specialized search and evidential aggregation. Across strong backbones, AUDITFLOW keeps EER near zero, while most remaining errors are CER, indicating that expected-value reconstruction is the main residual challenge. 5.2 Backbone Robustness We further report verdict-only accuracy in Table 1 to separate binary decision quality from full artifact correctness. Five LLMs obtain around 80% Joint ACC, but their VAcc remains above 94%. Thats because VAcc only checks the consistent/violation decision, whereas Joint ACC also requires the reBackbone Joint ACC VAcc Gap GPT-5.5 GPT-4o Sonnet 4.6 Qwen-397B Qwen-27B Fino1-14B 82.09 80.60 80.60 80.60 73.13 31.34 98.51 95.52 95.52 94.03 85.07 59.70 16.42 14.92 14.92 13.43 11.94 28.36 Table 1: AUDITFLOW backbone robustness. VAcc measures only the binary consistent/violation decision. Gap is VAcc minus Joint ACC (%), showing the difference between making the right decision and producing the full audit artifact. ported value and expected value to be correct. Performance drops with weaker backbones. Qwen-27B reaches 73.13% Joint ACC and 85.07% VAcc, while Fino1-14B falls to 31.34% Joint ACC and 59.70% VAcc. The larger gap for Fino1-14B suggests that it often reaches plausible binary decision but fails to produce complete audit artifact. Across stronger models, the main residual difficulty is expected-value reconstruction rather than reported-value extraction, as also reflected by the low EER and higher CER in Figure 3. The paired bootstrap tests are provided in Appendix and Appendix G. 5.3 Ablation Study Figure 4(a) ablates the main components of AUDITFLOW under GPT-5.5. Removing deterministic checks causes the largest drop: Joint ACC falls from 82.09% to 17.91%, invalid outputs increase from 1.49% to 35.82%, and accuracy on DQC.US.0117 and DQC.US.0126 drops to 0.00%. This gives the clearest evidence that the symbolic environment is not just retrieval substrate. Its rule-specific checkers perform the verification step that the model cannot reliably replace. The required-tool gate and ER fusion provide Figure 4: (a) Ablation results on GPT-5.5. Rule columns report per-rule Joint ACC; Invalid is the structurally unusable output rate. (b) Tool-use behavior across backbones. A1 uses static taxonomy tools only, so its static/dynamic split is omitted. A2 consistently mixes taxonomy and filing tools, while the tool budget varies by backbone. additional stability. Without the gate, Joint ACC drops to 74.63% and invalid outputs rise to 17.91%, suggesting that agents can finalize before collecting enough evidence. Without ER fusion, Joint ACC drops to 77.61%. Together, the ablations show that deterministic checks carry most of the verification burden, while the gate and evidential aggregation help produce more stable audit artifact. 5.4 Agent Behavior We next examine whether the two junior auditors follow different search policies rather than simply paraphrasing the same prompt. Figure 4(b) reports tool-use statistics across backbones. The Compliance Auditor A1 uses static taxonomy tools exclusively on every model, while the Forensic Auditor A2 consistently mixes static and dynamic tools. This shows that the persona split is preserved across backbones. The amount of tool use still varies by model: GPT-5.5 is the most exploratory, whereas the Qwen models use fewer tools on average. Figure 5 further shows the temporal pattern of A2s search. Across backbones, A2 starts in the dynamic filing environment and then shifts toward static taxonomy tools and mandatory deterministic checks. Thus, A2 follows an evidence-first, checkslast policy. What changes across models is how long this evidence phase lasts. GPT-5.5 stays in the dynamic phase the longest, while Qwen-27B switches earlier. The exception is Fino1-14B, which produces no well-formed tool calls in this analysis. This matches its lower performance in Section 5.1: the model often fails before reaching the deterministic checks. We also find that junior disagreement is useful signal of difficulty. On Qwen-27B, cases Figure 5: Temporal tool-use profile of the Forensic Auditor A2. Each point shows the share of dynamic filing-environment calls at tool step k. All backbones follow an evidence-first, checks-last pattern, with different switchover depths. Fino1-14B is omitted because it emits no well-formed tool calls. with pre-senior disagreement are much harder than agreement cases, with final accuracy dropping from 80.4% to 36.4% (see Appendix F.3).

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced AUDITFLOW, graph-grounded multi-agent framework for XBRL audit verification. AUDITFLOW separates search from computation: LLM agents guide evidence exploration, while symbolic environment performs fact retrieval, taxonomy traversal, numerical checking, and rule evaluation. Experiments on FinAuditingderived FinMR sample show that this design outperforms LLM-only, retrieval-based, and agentic baselines. Ablations further show that deterministic checks are the main source of reliability. These results suggest that reliable audit agents should be built around executable structured environments 8 rather than text-only reasoning.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our evaluation is limited to 67-instance subset of the FinMR task from FinAuditing and three DQC rule families. These cases cover important forms of numerical consistency verification, but they do not represent the full range of XBRL audit rules, filing types, companies, or reporting years. Future work should evaluate the framework on larger datasets and broader rule sets. AUDITFLOW also assumes that XBRL filings and US-GAAP taxonomy releases can be parsed into reliable structured graphs. This is suitable for tagged public-company filings, but it does not directly handle scanned reports, incomplete tags, or less structured financial disclosures. Extending the framework to those settings would require additional extraction and normalization steps. Although deterministic tools perform the loadbearing verification, the framework still depends on the LLM backbone to follow the tool protocol and produce usable reports. We observe that weaker models can fail before reaching the deterministic checks. In addition, our evaluation follows the FinAuditing LLM-as-a-judge protocol, which may introduce judge errors despite fixed configurations and caching. Finally, AUDITFLOW is intended as research system for audit verification and should not be treated as substitute for professional audit judgment without further validation. integration with existing audit and compliance procedures.

sections.title: Artifact Use and Licensing
sections.content: Our experiments use public-company filings retrieved from SEC EDGAR, released benchmark metadata from FinAuditing, and publicly available US-GAAP taxonomy releases. We use these resources for research evaluation and cite their original sources. We do not redistribute raw SEC filing packages or proprietary model outputs as part of this paper. Any released code or derived data will include references to the original resources and follow the applicable terms of use of the source artifacts. The fields used in our experiments contain company identifiers, filing metadata, audit queries, taxonomy concepts, reporting periods, and numerical ground-truth answers; they do not contain private personal information or user-generated offensive content. Public SEC filings may include publicly disclosed officer or signatory names, but these are not used as prediction targets or evaluation labels.

sections.title: Use of AI Assistants
sections.content: The authors used AI assistants, including ChatGPT, to support language polishing, brainstorming, and LaTeX editing. All technical claims, experimental results, analysis, and final writing decisions were reviewed and verified by the authors.

sections.title: References
sections.content: AUDITFLOW is designed as research system for XBRL audit verification, not as replacement for professional audit judgment. Because the task concerns financial filings, incorrect outputs could create risk if users treat the system as an authoritative auditor. false negative may miss an inconsistency, while false positive may raise unnecessary concern about valid filing. We reduce this risk by requiring evidence trails, deterministic rule checks, and explicit reported and expected values in the output, so that each verdict can be inspected rather than accepted as model-only judgment. The system uses public-company filing data and It benchmark labels derived from FinAuditing. does not trade, execute financial actions, or make regulatory decisions. sections.title: A Tool Access and Tool Inventory
sections.content: This appendix provides the full tool specification used by AUDITFLOW. In the main paper, we describe the tool interface as typed actionobservation layer. Here, we give the agent-specific tool access and the complete tool inventory. Agent-specific tool access. AUDITFLOW assigns tools according to the role of each agent. Table 2 summarizes the tool catalogs and terminal constraints. The Compliance Auditor A1 receives the static taxonomy catalog because its role is to verify whether the reported fact satisfies the relevant regulatory constraints. Its tools therefore focus on concept metadata, calculation trees, dimensional axes, DQC rule identification, and the three deterministic checkers. The Forensic Auditor A2 receives both static and dynamic tools. In addition to the taxonomy tools used by A1, it can inspect filing-specific evidence such as historical values, dimensional breakdowns, related concepts, sibling arithmetic, unit consistency, and magnitude patterns. This broader catalog allows A2 to start from the filing evidence and then connect that evidence back to taxonomy constraints. The Senior Auditor As does not call tools in the production setting. It reads the two junior reports and either accepts their agreement or returns targeted feedback when they disagree. We also implement ReAct senior for ablation, where As receives the full A2 catalog. This ablation tests whether allowing the senior to call tools changes the behavior of the framework, but it is not used in the main production setting. Required-tool constraints. The two junior auditors are allowed to choose their own search trajectories, but they cannot finalize or ask the senior for arbitration before completing the required deterministic checks. For A1, the required tools are check_sign, check_calc_tree, and check_dim_consistency. For A2, we additionally require get_fact_history, which ensures that the evidence-first path is exercised before the report is accepted. If junior auditor attempts to stop early, the environment rejects the terminal action and returns the missing tools as the next observation. This design keeps the search adaptive while preventing unsupported conclusions. Tool inventory. Table 3 lists all tools exposed by the audit environment. We group them into three categories. Static taxonomy tools expose the regulatory subspace, including concept definitions, calculation relationships, dimensional axes, and candidate DQC rules. Static check tools execute the terminal rule-specific verification steps for the three DQC rule families evaluated in this work. Dynamic filing tools expose filing-specific evidence used mainly by the Forensic Auditor, including historical facts, period comparisons, dimensional breakdowns, sibling relationships, and unit or decimal consistency. The distinction between static and dynamic tools mirrors the dual-graph environment. Static tools operate over the US-GAAP taxonomy graph and rule-specific constraint structure. Dynamic tools operate over the filing graph and recover the evidence needed to verify concrete reported value. The deterministic checkers connect these two views by applying the relevant DQC rule to reported facts under the governing taxonomy constraints. Role of deterministic checkers. The three check_-prefixed tools are the terminal verification tools used by the system. They are not ordinary retrieval operations. Each checker returns structured rule outcome and the audit-critical values needed by the evaluator, including the reported value, expected value when applicable, supporting facts, and rule-specific findings. These outputs are treated as authoritative observations in the production pipeline. The LLM may decide when to invoke checker and how to use its observation, but it cannot replace the checker result with selfgenerated conclusion. This is the main mechanism by which AUDITFLOW separates adaptive search from deterministic verification.

sections.title: B Dataset Details
sections.content: We use 67-instance subset of the FinMR task from FinAuditing (Wang et al., 2026). Each instance specifies an audit query, filing type, DQC rule, ticker, filing release date, target US-GAAP concept, reporting period, and ground-truth answer. The ground truth contains the reported value to be extracted from the filing and the expected value computed from the relevant taxonomy and linkbase evidence. Table 4 summarizes the CSV schema. The dataset covers three DQC rule families: DQC.US.0015, DQC.US.0117, and DQC.US.0126. The 67 instances include 22 DQC.US.0015 cases, 24 DQC.US.0117 cases, and 21 DQC.US.0126 12 Agent Catalog Tools A1 Compliance Static taxonomy A2 Forensic Static + dynamic lookup_concept_spec; list_dim_axes; check_calc_tree; get_fact find_dqc_rules; walk_calc_tree; check_sign; check_dim_consistency; get_fact_history; plus detect_temporal_outlier; A1 All tools, compare_periods; get_dimensional_breakdown; compare_dim_members; check_sibling_math_consistency; check_magnitude_plausibility; check_unit_decimal_consistency; find_related_concepts Required before terminal action check_sign; check_calc_tree; check_dim_consistency get_fact_history; check_sign; check_calc_tree; check_dim_consistency As Senior Production: none; ReAct ablation: static + dynamic Production senior reads junior reports and emits feedback or acceptance. In ReAct ablation mode, As receives the full A2 catalog. Production: not applicable; ReAct ablation: no required-tools gate Table 2: Agent-specific tool access in AUDITFLOW. The two junior auditors use the same ReAct engine but different tool catalogs and terminal constraints. Required tools must return non-error observations before junior auditor may finalize or ask the senior for arbitration. cases. The filings include both annual and quarterly reports, with 29 10-K filings and 38 10-Q filings, covering 47 unique tickers. For evaluation, we use the released FinAuditing metadata to identify the target ticker and audit query, then retrieve the corresponding raw XBRL filing package from SEC EDGAR. We also parse the relevant US-GAAP taxonomy release independently. The raw filing is used to build the dynamic filing graph, while the taxonomy release is used to build the static regulatory graph. This setup avoids evaluating only on pre-extracted textual evidence and requires the system to perform verification over structured filing and taxonomy artifacts.

sections.title: C Model Details
sections.content: Table 5 lists the backbone models used in our experiments. We include both proprietary API models and open-weight or hosted models to test whether AUDITFLOW depends on single model family. For proprietary models, parameter scale is not publicly disclosed. For open-weight models, we report the released model scale.

sections.title: D Evaluation Metric Details
sections.content: This appendix provides the full definitions of the evaluation metrics used in our experiments. The main paper reports the core audit metrics, including Joint ACC, VAcc, SER, EER, and CER. Here, we describe the judge protocol, metric formulas, grounded interaction metrics, and model-invariance metrics. , ˆvexp is the reported value, ˆvexp LLM-as-a-judge protocol. For each case i, the system outputs an audit artifact zi = (ˆyi, ˆvrep , ˆRi, ˆPi), where ˆyi is the binary veri dict, ˆvrep is the expected value, ˆRi is the set of triggered rules, and ˆPi is the reasoning path. Following FinAuditing (Wang et al., 2026), an LLM-as-a-judge compares this artifact with the benchmark reference and assigns one mutually exclusive label: Accurate (A), Structural (S), Extraction (E), or Calculation (C). case is labeled Accurate only when the audit output is usable and the audit-critical values are correct. Structural error means the output is malformed, incomplete, or unusable as an audit artifact. An Extraction error means the system recovers the reported fact or reported value incorrectly. Calculation error means the system reaches valid structure and extracts the reported value correctly, but produces an incorrect expected value or supporting calculation. Joint audit accuracy. The main metric is joint audit accuracy: ACC = 1 N (cid:88) i=1 1[ℓi = A], (10) where ℓi is the judge label for case i. This metric is stricter than ordinary binary accuracy because the full audit artifact must be correct, not only the consistent/violation decision. Tool group Tool Observation returned to the agent Static taxonomy lookup_concept_spec Static taxonomy walk_calc_tree Static taxonomy Static taxonomy list_dim_axes find_dqc_rules Static check check_sign Static check check_calc_tree Static check check_dim_consistency Static taxonomy get_fact Dynamic filing get_fact_history Dynamic filing compare_periods Dynamic filing Dynamic filing Dynamic filing detect_temporal_outlier get_dimensional_breakdown compare_dim_members Concept metadata: balance, period type, data type, label, definition, and abstract flag. Weighted calculation-linkbase neighborhood, upward or downward, around the target concept. Dimensional axes associated with the target concept. Conservative list of potentially applicable DQC rule families inferred from taxonomy structure. DQC.US.0015 verdict over non-dimensional and dimensional facts, including the offending value and balance attribute when violation is found. DQC.US.0126 verdict, expected value vexp, and weighted child-sum breakdown. DQC.US.0117 verdict, non-dimensional total, candidate dimensional sums, dimensional facts, and ambiguity flag. All reported facts for concept and period, including value, unit, decimals, period, and dimensional context. Filing-internal comparative periods for the same concept, or no-history signal. Delta and percentage change between two reported periods of the same concept. Temporal anomaly signal against filing-internal history. Dimensional facts grouped by unit and axis tuple. Member shares, dimensional sum, and match against the nondimensional total. Dynamic filing check_sibling_math_consistency Sibling arithmetic around parents that contain the target conDynamic filing check_magnitude_plausibility cept in the filing calculation tree. Order-of-magnitude anomaly signal relative to other nondimensional facts in the same filing. Dynamic filing Dynamic filing check_unit_decimal_consistency Unit and decimals consistency checks for all target facts. find_related_concepts Parents, children, and siblings structurally adjacent to the target concept inside the filing graph. Table 3: Complete tool inventory. Static tools expose the regulatory subspace and deterministic DQC checks; dynamic tools expose filing-specific evidence used by the Forensic Auditor. Field Description Model Repository / API name Scale Instance identifier. Natural-language audit query. Filing type, such as 10-K or 10-Q. DQC rule identifier. Company ticker used to retrieve the filing. Filing release date. id query filing_name dqc_rule ticker issue_time usgaap_concept Target taxonomy concept to audit. period Reporting period of the target fact. gt_answer Ground-truth reported value and expected value. Table 4: Schema of the FinMR audit subset used in our experiments. Per-rule accuracy. We also compute Joint ACC for each DQC rule family: ACCr = 1 Cr (cid:88) iCr 1[ℓi = A], (11) where Cr is the subset of cases governed by rule family {0015, 0117, 0126}. This breakdown is useful because the three rule families stress different capabilities: sign metadata lookup, dimensional aggregation, and calculation-linkbase arithmetic. Verdict-only accuracy. To separate decision quality from numerical artifact quality, we report 14 GPT-5.5 (OpenAI, 2026) GPT-4o (Hurst et al., 2024) Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic, 2026) Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (Qwen Team, 2026a) Qwen/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B 397B 27B Qwen3.6-27B (Qwen Team, 2026b) 14B Fino1-14B (Qian et al., 2025) gpt-5.5 gpt-4o-2024-08-06 claude-sonnet-4Qwen/Qwen3.6-27B TheFinAI/Fino1-14B Not disclosed Not disclosed Not disclosed Table 5: Backbone models used in our experiments. Not disclosed indicates that the provider does not publicly release the parameter scale. verdict-only accuracy: VAcc = 1 N (cid:88) i=1 1[ˆyi = yi]. (12) This metric ignores expected-value errors and measures whether the system predicts the correct consistent/violation outcome. Diagnostic error rates. We report the three nonaccurate judge-label rates: Errq = 1 i=1 (cid:88) 1[ℓi = q], {S, E, C}. (13) These correspond to structural error rate (SER), extraction error rate (EER), and calculation error rate (CER). They separate output-format failures, reported-value retrieval failures, and numericalreconstruction failures. Grounded interaction metrics. For the agentic process, we report the mean number of ReAct steps per case, parse failures, required-tool gate rejections, and senior-feedback rounds. These metrics measure whether the backbone can operate the action schema and whether the required-tools gate is actually invoked. We also report statictool share, dynamic-tool share, and static-dynamic graph crossings for each junior auditor. These trajectory metrics test whether the Compliance and Forensic auditors implement distinct policies over the dual-graph environment rather than producing two paraphrases of the same prompt. Inter-agent disagreement. We report the ER conflict mass between the two junior reports. High conflict means that the two auditors reached incompatible structured conclusions before final fusion. We use this value as difficulty signal in the analysis.

sections.title: E Implementation Details
sections.content: Software stack. AUDITFLOW is implemented in Python as an executable audit pipeline rather than prompt-only benchmark. The graph layer is built with networkx; XBRL filings are parsed with lxml; and tabular filing artifacts are handled with openpyxl. The agent layer uses langgraph and langchain abstractions, with provider adapters for OpenAI, Anthropic, Together, Fireworks, OpenRouter, local OpenAI-compatible servers, and HuggingFace Transformers. The implementation keeps the audit environment, tool interface, LLM backend, evidential aggregation, and evaluation code separate, so changing backbone does not change the graph construction, deterministic checkers, ER fusion, or judge pipeline. Model configuration. Unless otherwise stated, single backbone is used uniformly for all three agents in run: the Compliance Auditor A1, the Forensic Auditor A2, and the Senior Auditor As. For OpenAI-compatible standard models, we use greedy decoding with temperature 0 and 4096token output budget for junior auditors. The senior auditor receives an 8192-token budget because it reads both junior reports. For OpenAI reasoning-family models, we use the providers reasoning interface, with reasoning_effort=low for audit agents when applicable, and do not The offline pass temperature parameters. LLM-as-a-judge is fixed across all runs as gpt-5-mini with reasoning_effort=minimal, verbosity=low, and 256-token output budget. Interaction budgets. Each junior auditor may take at most 20 ReAct tool steps before the system force-finalizes from the accumulated deterministic observations. The senior-feedback loop is capped at 3 rounds. In the production setting, the senior is single-shot arbitrator over junior reports. ReAct senior with 10-step budget is implemented only for ablation. Main evaluations are run asynchronously with up to 8 concurrent audit cases. Local and remote inference. Commercial backbones are accessed through hosted APIs. Opensource backbones can be served through OpenAIcompatible local servers, including vllm, sglang, or transformers serve, or through an in-process HuggingFace backend. Local-model experiments were run on 8 GPU nodes with two NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The local PyTorch stack used torch 2.9.0+cu128; model loading used HuggingFace transformers with accelerate and automatic device mapping when running in process. Reproducibility and caching. The pipeline writes one JSONL audit artifact per run and companion trajectory sidecar containing per-agent tool calls, observations, parse failures, gate rejections, and finalization status. LLM calls are cached on disk with keys that include the role, model name, system prompt, canonical message history, and sorted tool names. The judge is cached separately. Replaying an unchanged run against warm cache reproduces the same audit artifacts and judge labels without additional LLM calls.

sections.title: F Full Experimental Results
sections.content: This appendix reports the full method-by-model results for all baselines and all backbone models. The main paper reports the primary backbone comparison and the AUDITFLOW backbone analysis. Here, we provide the complete Joint ACC matrix and the corresponding diagnostic error rates. F.1 Full Joint ACC Table 7 reports Joint ACC for all method and backbone combinations. AUDITFLOW achieves the best result under every backbone. The gain is consistent across both proprietary and open-weight 15 Metric family Audit artifact Metric Joint ACC Audit artifact Per-rule ACC Audit artifact Verdict-only ACC Error diagnosis Error diagnosis Error diagnosis Tool-use process SER EER CER Steps/case; parse failures; gate rejections; seniorfeedback rounds Environment trajectory Static share; dynamic Inter-agent evidence Model invariance share; graph crossings Conflict mass Decision; Extracted; Computed; Judge Rules; What it measures Percentage of cases labeled Accurate by the judge; requires valid audit artifact with correct reported and expected values. Joint ACC computed separately for DQC.US.0015, DQC.US.0117, and DQC.US.0126. Binary consistent/violation accuracy, ignoring expected-value mistakes. Structural error rate; malformed, incomplete, or unusable audit output. Extraction error rate; incorrect reported fact or reported value recovered from the filing. Calculation error rate; valid structure and correct extraction, but incorrect expected value or supporting calculation. Operational competence of the ReAct protocol and the requiredtools gate. Whether agents explore the static taxonomy and dynamic filing environments according to their assigned roles. Degree of disagreement between junior auditors before final ER fusion. Case-level divergence from the GPT-4o reference run, used to test whether capable backbones converge to the same audit artifact. Table 6: Full evaluation metrics used in the experiments. The first families evaluate the final audit artifact; the remaining families evaluate whether the artifact was produced through grounded, inspectable interaction with the dual-graph environment. Method GPT-5.5 GPT-4o Sonnet 4.6 Qwen-397B Qwen-27B Fino1-14B 14.93 FinAuditing 7.46 Direct LLM 7.46 Vanilla RAG 48.39 GraphRAG 43.28 TreeRAG 65.67 Herculean Single Agent 67.16 AUDITFLOW 82.09 5.97 5.97 1.49 38.10 35.82 65.67 67.16 80.60 7.46 1.49 2.99 46.77 41.79 65.67 67.16 80. 5.97 8.96 4.48 27.42 32.84 43.28 67.16 80.60 4.48 1.49 7.46 22.39 23.88 37.31 64.18 73.13 14.93 7.46 1.49 17.91 20.90 28.35 29.85 31.34 Table 7: Full Joint ACC (%) comparison across methods and backbone models. models. Under GPT-5.5, AUDITFLOW reaches 82.09%, compared with 67.16% for Single Agent and 65.67% for Herculean. The same pattern holds for GPT-4o, Sonnet 4.6, Qwen-397B, Qwen-27B, and Fino1-14B. The results also show clear separation between retrieval-based baselines and executable tool-based systems. GraphRAG and TreeRAG improve over Direct LLM and Vanilla RAG, which suggests that structured context is useful. However, they remain below the agentic systems because the final verification still depends on the models own rule application and numerical reasoning. Single Agent improves further by using the same dual-graph environment and deterministic tools as AUDITFLOW. AUDITFLOW gives the best results by adding rolespecialized search and report aggregation on top of the same environment. 16 F.2 Full Diagnostic Error Rates Table 8 reports SER, EER, and CER for all method and backbone combinations. The error profiles clarify why the methods differ. Direct LLM and Vanilla RAG often suffer from high structural error rates, especially when the input context is long or weakly organized. This indicates that these methods often fail before reaching the numerical verification step. GraphRAG and TreeRAG reduce structural failures for stronger backbones, but they still show substantial calculation errors, which means that retrieved structure alone does not eliminate the need for executable checking. The agentic systems have lower structural error rates and better Joint ACC, but their error profiles differ. Single Agent reduces many failures compared with retrieval-based methods, yet it still has higher CER than AUDITFLOW under the strongest backbone. AUDITFLOW keeps EER near zero across most backbones, which shows that it usually retrieves the reported value correctly. Its remaining errors are mainly CER, indicating that the hardest cases are those requiring exact expected-value reconstruction. This pattern is consistent with the main finding: deterministic tools improve reliability, but complex calculation and dimensional cases remain the primary source of residual errors. Metric Method GPT-5.5 GPT-4o Sonnet 4.6 Qwen-397B Qwen-27B Fino1-14B Backbone Joint ACC (%) Correct 95% CI GPT-4o GPT-5.5 Claude-Sonnet-4.6 Qwen3.5-397B-A17B Qwen3.6-27B Fino1-14B 80.60 82.09 80.60 80. 73.13 31.34 54/67 55/67 54/67 54/67 49/67 12/67 [70.1, 89.6] [75.8, 90.2] [70.1, 89.6] [70.1, 89.6] [62.7, 83.6] [26.9, 40.6] Table 10: Per-backbone Joint ACC with 95% percentile bootstrap confidence intervals over 67 cases and = 104 resamples. ACC 95% CI [8.8, +5.8] 1.5 GPT-5.5 GPT-4o [0, 0] +0.0 Claude-Sonnet-4.6 GPT-4o [0, 0] Qwen3.5-397B-A17B +0.0 GPT-4o +7.5 [+1.5, +14.9] Qwen3.6-27B GPT-4o +49.3 [+26.5, +73.1] < 0.001 GPT-4o Fino1-14B +41.7 [+23.3, +67.2] < 0.001 Qwen3.6-27B Fino1-14B > 0.5 1.000 1.000 0.032 Table 11: Paired bootstrap comparisons on Joint ACC. ACC is ACC(A)ACC(B) in percentage points. We use = 104 case-level resamples and two-sided recentred bootstrap test. tor in this run, so they share the same Joint ACC and confidence interval. Qwen3.6-27B forms lower tier, and Fino1-14B is substantially lower. The intervals are relatively wide because the evaluation set has 67 cases, but the overall tiered pattern remains clear. Table 11 reports paired bootstrap comparisons. GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Qwen3.5-397BA17B are identical at the case level in this run, giving = 0 and = 1 when compared with GPT-4o. GPT-5.5 differs from GPT-4o by only 1.5 points, which corresponds to one case out of 67, and the paired bootstrap interval includes zero ([8.8, +5.8]). We therefore do not treat this gap as statistically meaningful. By contrast, the drop from GPT-4o to Qwen3.6-27B is significant (+7.5 points, = 0.032), and the drop to Fino1-14B is much larger (+49.3 points, < 0.001). These results support the backbone analysis in the main paper: capable backbones produce similar audit outcomes, whereas weaker backbones exhibit statistically significant degradation. Junior reports Cases Final ACC Mean conflict Agree Disagree 56 11 80.4% 36.4% 0.000 0.330 Table 9: Cases grouped by whether the two juniors agree before senior adjudication, measured on Qwen3.6-27B. Disagreement cases are substantially harder and have higher ER conflict mass. F. Inter-Agent Disagreement We further analyze whether disagreement between the two junior auditors marks difficult cases. Table 9 groups Qwen3.6-27B cases by whether the two junior reports agree before senior adjudication. When the juniors agree, the final ACC is 80.4%. When they disagree, the final ACC drops to 36.4%, and the mean conflict mass rises to 0.330. This shows that disagreement is not random variation between two prompts. It identifies cases where the evidence is harder to reconcile before final aggregation.

sections.title: Comparisons
sections.content: We use case-level paired bootstrap tests to assess whether the backbone-level differences in Joint ACC are robust to the finite size of the 67-case evaluation set. For each model, we resample the 67 cases with replacement for = 104 bootstrap samples and recompute Joint ACC. For pairwise comparisons, we resample paired correctness indicators and report ACC, 95% confidence intervals, and two-sided recentred bootstrap p-values following (Berg-Kirkpatrick et al., 2012). Table 10 reports per-backbone Joint ACC with bootstrap confidence intervals. The four strongest backbones have the same per-case correctness vec-
